narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, estimated a 96.9% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with strong odds of bar success to maintain high pass rates. The model's decision for you, as a Black male applicant, hinged most on your academic rankings from earlier years in school, your LSAT score, and your first-year decile performance. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. This low ranking signaled to the model a concerning dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 26, which is substantially below the average of about 37 seen in the dataset, further tilting the prediction toward failure by highlighting potential gaps in test-taking readiness for the bar. Your first-year decile of 1, also in the bottom 10% compared to the average around the 6th decile, added to this by reinforcing an early pattern of underperformance relative to peers. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 exceeds the typical 3.21 average and your middle family income bracket aligns with many applicants, along with planning full-time study like over 90% of students, these did not outweigh the top concerns. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows weaker early academic deciles and LSAT, which are red flags the model weighs heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above 37, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or tutoring, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that address these areas. Reapplying after addressing these key factors could significantly improve your odds in the model's eyes and open doors at other programs. Keep pushing—you have strengths like your solid UGPA that show promise with targeted improvements.
